title: The Nervous System and Addiction: Why Substances Regulate What Trauma Dysregulates
excerpt: Explore how trauma dysregulates the nervous system and why substances often become a way to self-regulate, with insights from leading trauma researchers.
body:
Introduction: Addiction Through the Lens of Nervous System Regulation

Many people understand addiction as a behavioral or moral failing, or simply a habit gone wrong. But decades of research reveal a much deeper story: addiction is fundamentally tied to how our nervous system responds to trauma and stress. When trauma dysregulates the nervous system, substances and behaviors often become a way to restore balance, at least temporarily. This article unpacks the neurobiological roots of addiction through the lens of trauma and somatic regulation, drawing on the groundbreaking work of Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, Stephen Porges, Deb Dana, and others.

How Trauma Dysregulates the Nervous System

Trauma—whether from early childhood neglect, abuse, or overwhelming life events—leaves an imprint on the nervous system. Bessel van der Kolk, in his seminal book The Body Keeps the Score, explains that trauma disrupts our innate capacity for nervous system regulation. Instead of feeling safe and grounded, the nervous system becomes stuck in states of hyperarousal (fight/flight) or hypoarousal (freeze/shutdown). This chronic dysregulation impacts not only emotional health but also physical sensations, cognition, and behavior.

Peter Levine’s work with Somatic Experiencing highlights that trauma is held in the body as incomplete defensive responses that never fully discharged. These unresolved states keep the nervous system locked in survival mode. The result is an ongoing sense of threat, even when the original danger has passed.

The Polyvagal Perspective: Understanding Safety and Connection

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory provides a roadmap for understanding how the autonomic nervous system (ANS) shifts between states of safety and defense. The vagus nerve, a key player in the parasympathetic system, has two branches: the ventral vagal (social engagement, safety) and dorsal vagal (shutdown, immobilization). Trauma often pushes the nervous system toward dorsal vagal dominance or sympathetic hyperarousal, disrupting social connection and self-regulation.

“Addiction can be understood as a desperate attempt by the nervous system to recreate a feeling of safety and calm that trauma has taken away.” – The Oracle Lover

Why Substances and Behaviors Become Tools for Nervous System Regulation

When the nervous system is dysregulated, substances like alcohol, opioids, stimulants, or even behaviors such as compulsive eating or gambling, serve an important biological function: they help modulate overwhelming internal states. Gabor Maté, a leading expert on addiction and trauma, emphasizes that addiction is not about pleasure alone but about pain management—self-medicating to soothe unbearable internal distress.

Neurochemically, many addictive substances temporarily increase neurotransmitters like dopamine, serotonin, or endorphins, which can recreate sensations of safety, pleasure, or calm. But these effects are fleeting, and the body soon craves more to maintain the illusion of relief. Without addressing the underlying trauma and nervous system dysregulation, these cycles often become chronic.

Somatic Healing Approaches to Addiction Recovery

Traditional talk therapy can fall short for many people with addiction because it often addresses thoughts and behaviors without engaging the body’s nervous system directly. Somatic-based therapies, by contrast, focus on helping clients notice, track, and regulate bodily sensations to restore autonomic balance.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy and Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal-informed interventions emphasize tools such as mindful breathing, grounding exercises, and tracking subtle shifts in bodily states. These approaches build capacity for nervous system regulation, reducing the need to rely on substances as external regulators.

Internal Family Systems and Addiction

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic work by helping people identify and work with internal parts that hold trauma, pain, and addictive urges. Addiction often involves protecting parts that fear vulnerability or overwhelm. IFS offers a compassionate way to access and heal these internal dynamics, supporting nervous system integration.

Why Understanding Addiction as Nervous System Dysregulation Changes the Narrative

Viewing addiction through this lens shifts responsibility away from blame and towards compassion and science. It explains why willpower alone rarely leads to lasting recovery. Instead, healing requires creating safety and learning to regulate the nervous system differently.

Dan Siegel’s concept of mindsight—the ability to perceive internal states with awareness—helps individuals interrupt reactive patterns and develop new neural pathways. This neural integration is key to rewiring the brain’s responses to stress and craving.

Practical Steps to Support Nervous System Regulation in Recovery

  • Body Awareness: Practice somatic tracking—notice sensations without judgment.
  • Breathing Techniques: Engage in slow, deep breathing to stimulate the ventral vagal complex.
  • Safe Social Connection: Cultivate relationships that feel safe and supportive.
  • Movement: Gentle yoga, walking, or dance to discharge trapped energy.
  • Mindfulness and Meditation: Develop mindsight through regular practice.
  • Professional Support: Work with somatic therapists trained in trauma and addiction.

Conclusion: Healing Addiction Through Nervous System Regulation

Addiction is much more than a bad habit—it is a profound nervous system struggle rooted in trauma. Understanding why substances and behaviors temporarily regulate what trauma dysregulates opens a path to compassionate, body-based healing. By focusing on restoring nervous system balance through somatic awareness, social engagement, and integration, people can reclaim resilience and freedom beyond addiction.

As Bessel van der Kolk reminds us, "The challenge of recovery is not just to understand trauma cognitively, but to feel safe in one’s body again." Addiction recovery is possible when the nervous system learns it can be safe once more.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Penguin Books.
  • Levine, P. (1997). Waking the Tiger: Healing Trauma. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Maté, G. (2010). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. Norton Series on Interpersonal Neurobiology.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Siegel, D. (2010). Mind: A Journey to the Heart of Being Human. W.W. Norton & Company.

faqs.a: Trauma dysregulates the nervous system, creating states of hyperarousal or shutdown. Addiction often emerges as a way to self-regulate these overwhelming internal states.

faqs.q: Why do substances provide temporary relief from trauma symptoms?
faqs.a: Addictive substances temporarily alter neurochemicals like dopamine and serotonin, creating a fleeting sense of safety or calm that trauma has disrupted.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapies help in addiction recovery?
faqs.a: Yes, somatic therapies engage the body directly to restore nervous system balance, reducing the need to rely on substances for regulation.

faqs.q: What is the role of social connection in nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: Safe social engagement activates the ventral vagal system, promoting feelings of safety and helping regulate the nervous system away from defensive states.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: Essential reading for understanding trauma’s impact on the body and mind—a foundational resource for somatic healing.

metaTitle: Nervous System and Addiction: Trauma's Hidden Link
metaDescription: Discover why addiction regulates what trauma dysregulates in the nervous system and how somatic healing supports lasting recovery.
author: The Oracle Lover
